summary.brief: Grano v. Keating. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Dr. Keating was Decedent’s primary care provider from 2007 to 2015. In the spring of 2015, Decedent saw Dr. Keating for a mass in her breast, after which Dr. Keating ordered a mammogram and a CT scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Hebert, a radiologist, analyzed the scan. Believing the mass to be a post-traumatic abnormality, Dr. Hebert suggested a follow up CT scan six months later and advised that it was unnecessary to perform a biopsy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On November 9, 2015, another CT scan was done, which Dr. Hebert also analyzed and compared to the scan from the spring. Although the lump had grown, he reaffirmed his conclusion from the spring and advised that further sampling was unnecessary.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On December 3, 2015, Dr. Keating discussed the results with Decedent. Dr. Keating suggested further discussion of the results during Decedent’s annual checkup, which was scheduled for mid-January 2016.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In early January, Decedent canceled the checkup, and on March 11, 2016, she had her first visit with a new primary care physician, who subsequently ordered CT scans, and a biopsy of the mass was ordered by another physician.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The results showed the mass had grown since the second CT scan. The mass was surgically removed and diagnosed as cancerous on July 12, 2016.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Over two years later, on September 7, 2018, Decedent passed away. Her death was caused by the cancer.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court applied the general rule and declined to apply the exception. The court therefore dismissed as time-barred claims for personal injury resulting in wrongful death filed by Plaintiff Marc Grano.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We hold that the district court did not err by entering summary judgment on the Estate’s claims for wrongful death based on medical malpractice, which accrued when Decedent discovered her injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We affirm the dismissal of the wrongful death claims, reverse the dismissal of the loss of consortium claims, and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: In the spring of 2015, Decedent saw Dr. Keating for a mass in her breast.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Breast mass - differential includes post-traumatic abnormality

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scan to evaluate breast mass

reveal.result_summary: Mass believed to be post-traumatic abnormality
reveal.clinical_significance: Suggested follow-up CT scan in six months

key_events.event: CT scan analyzed by Dr. Hebert

decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Order biopsy of the breast mass
options.clinical_reasoning: Biopsy is necessary to rule out malignancy

options.description: Recommend follow-up CT scan in six months
options.clinical_reasoning: Believed mass to be post-traumatic abnormality

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant recommended follow-up CT scan in six months
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed mass to be post-traumatic abnormality

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established biopsy is standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order biopsy delayed diagnosis

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed cancer and passed away

key_findings: Claims were time-barred under the statute of repose

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Biopsy would have confirmed cancer earlier

tests_performed.result: Mass believed to be post-traumatic abnormality

court_findings.finding: Claims were time-barred under the statute of repose
